data.title: Watch Out, CCIA Is Getting Called
data.description:
Thesis

We own the Series A preferred shares due 2028 Carlyle Credit Income Fund NT 28 (NYSE:CCIA), and have covered it several times on the Seeking Alpha platform. In our last article we highlighted for readers why we thought the note was fairly priced, after retracing overbought conditions.

In today's piece we are going to discuss the latest corporate action relating to CCIA, and highlight for investors the best course of action.

What is happening? CCIA is getting called

In a SEC filing from October 3, 2025, the fund has disclosed its intent to call the term preferred shares:

- Title of the class of securities of Carlyle Credit Income Fund (the “Fund”) to be redeemed: 8.75% Series A Preferred Shares Due 2028 (CUSIP: 92535C 500) (the “Series A Preferred Shares”).

- Date on which the securities are to be redeemed: The Series A Preferred Shares will be redeemed on November 3, 2025 (the “Redemption Date”), subject to the satisfaction of the condition precedent that the Fund completes one or more financing transactions that generate net proceeds of at least $50 million (the “Financing Transactions”) prior to the Redemption Date, which condition precedent may be waived by the Fund in its sole discretion.

- The principal amount or number of shares and the basis upon which the securities to be redeemed are to be selected: On the Redemption Date, the Fund will redeem all of the outstanding Series A Preferred Shares (2,080,000 Series A Preferred Shares) pursuant to the terms of the Supplement, subject to the completion of the Financing Transactions or the waiver of such condition precedent by the Fund in its sole discretion.

Interesting to note that the redemption date is not even a payment date, with the scheduled monthly payment on November 17, 2025 as per the Seeking Alpha 'Dividend History' tab. As a reminder, the first call date for the securities is October 31, 2025, so the fund is calling the notes on the first possible date.

This should remind investors about the risks associated with buying a term preferred security significantly above par. Also interesting to note the fund is doing a new financing, but it is unclear whether it will be via a private offering or new baby bonds.

What will investors get?

Upon the maturity date, investors will get the $25/share par back, plus roughly half a month of accrued interest. Given the fund has paid $0.18 per month, investors will get roughly $25.09 per share upon maturity. Please note there is another payment date on October 21st, 2025 which will happen as scheduled. So net-net, from today to maturity date investors will get $25.27 per share. The notes have now decreased in value below this figure, with the market pricing in the call date.

Interesting to note that the yield to call for CCIA did matter, and the fund managed to obtain better financing after establishing a track record. Re-financings are not cheap, usually costing 50 to 75 bps, thus we presume the new facility will have a lower coupon.

What does this mean for you as an investor?

Given the corporate action CCIA has become a very short term security, with only one month of duration remaining. This means that as an investor you will get a big cash lump sum in November 2025, and one needs to find suitable replacements. If you are de-risking your portfolio this action will fit right in, since the cash balance in your portfolio is decreasing.

Since the price of the security is currently above par, there is little in terms of arbitrage opportunities to buy CCIA and getting full principal back in a month.

What does this mean for the CEF?

We were also wondering if the CEF is now de-leveraging via this redemption and refinancing, but it does not seem to be the case. Let us have a look:

As of the last balance sheet, the CEF had $73 million in preferred shares, accounting for most of the liabilities. Series A came up to $50 million of that figure, and the corporate action indicates they are getting a new $50 million financing. The leverage thus will not change, just the net cost to the fund, which is expected to decrease. Again, we are quite interested to see if the new financing will be done via the capital markets or private ones.

Asset class 'take-aways'

Investors should realize that CEF preferred shares are a niche category that acts more like bonds since they are liabilities on the balance sheet. As such term preferred shares get called, especially when coming with high coupons. When purchasing such securities investors should run two types of yield:

  • yield to maturity
  • yield to call

If the security's yield to call is too low, don't purchase it since it might get called and you will sit on a very low yield for the risk taken. Do not forget CCIA was trading at a massive 7% premium to par in February 2025, thus investors who bought there basically made nothing since. Zero percent return versus 4% in money markets. Analytics do matter, especially when it comes to term preferred securities from CEFs.

Conclusion

CCIA are term preferred shares from Carlyle Credit Income Fund (CCIF). As per a recent SEC filing the securities are getting called on November 3, 2025. On the call date the holders will get par plus accrued interest since the last payment date. The CEF has disclosed it has a pending financing arrangement that will replace the leverage provided by CCIA.

data.title: 2 Big Mistakes That Could Wreck Your Retirement
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Adulthood is often marked by the need to independently manage our requirements, including earning a living and managing expenses. A typical adult career spans between 40-50 years, often beginning in the early 20s until the decision to retire in the early 60s. Over the years, our expenses have varied significantly. Young adults need less space and resources to call a place home, often sharing to keep expenses light. As we age, our aspirations and ambitions grow, and family responsibilities tend to increase our expenses. Career growth is supposed to offset higher expenses, positioning the individual to save more for their retirement.

Throughout our adult lives, there are numerous expenses that come out of nowhere. It could be unexpected repair work in the house or vehicle, or a complete vehicle replacement, or some self- or family health-related expenses. There could also be recessions that create catastrophic losses for an individual’s portfolio (we discussed this in a recent article; no matter what kind of investor you are, you cannot avoid catastrophic losses). But most individuals can weather these storms through the cushion of regular earnings, allowing us to crawl out of the situation and regain financial standing.

Your retirement expenses will be substantial – and many of them will still catch you off guard. As such, you will be making withdrawals from your portfolio to handle them, and your portfolio will continue to be subject to market risks. Let us now discuss two common risks that retirees tend to ignore in their planning, which have the potential to cause significant hardship or negative impact on their lifestyle in retirement.

1. Sequence of Return Risk

Most investors don’t understand this concept and assume that time in the market is the cure for all volatility and bear markets. While time in the market is important for solid long-term returns, it isn’t always the case when regular withdrawals are made. Your exposure to sequence of return risk is based on the timing of market declines relative to when you begin making withdrawals. If the market declines when you start withdrawing income, you will be left with fewer dollars working to achieve growth and more frequent withdrawals will worsen the situation.

Look at the example below, showing two investors, A and B, retiring with identical portfolios worth $1 million, invested in the S&P 500. They believe all they need is $60,000 in annual income in retirement, withdrawn monthly. The only difference in their strategies is their chosen time of retirement. Investor A decided to retire at the end of 1997, making her first withdrawal in January 1998, while Investor B decided to retire in July and made his first withdrawal in August 1998. It doesn’t sound that different – Right?

Wrong. It resulted in vastly different circumstances. Investor A saw her portfolio gain in value in the first six months, followed by the 1998 market crash due to global economic challenges. Unfortunately, Investor B’s timing wasn’t that great, seeing an over 10% drop immediately. Apart from this "bump in the road," both saw their portfolios boom in the early years of the tech bubble, followed by a steep decline with the bust. The portfolios never recovered at a rate that matched their withdrawals, and the Great Financial Crisis accelerated the problems. Following the GFC, the U.S. economy recovered very slowly, and A's and B’s consistent withdrawals caused significant anxiety about running out of money. In just 17 years into their retirement, Investor B depleted their entire portfolio, while Investor A was given a longer lifeline by the bull market since 2015. Source.

Investor A and Investor B only retired six months apart, but the timing of the market crash became a game-changer. The sequence of return risk is real, and its occurrence is unpredictable. Dividends can help mitigate sequence of return risk by providing a consistent income stream, allowing investors not to have to sell shares in declining markets.

Let us take a look at Investor C, who built a portfolio like we do at HDO. While many of our holdings didn’t exist over this time period, we will look at an equal-weighted portfolio of four rather diverse holdings – Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), John Hancock Financial Opportunities Fund (BTO), Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), and abrdn Healthcare Investors (HQH). These have experienced significant impact from changing interest rates, wars, economic conditions, healthcare regulations, financial crises, and oil crashes during the 25-plus years.

Yet, collectively, this portfolio composition has massively outperformed the index while generating income that hugely exceeds the $60,000 planned withdrawals. Excess income allowed Investor C to prudently reinvest to buy more shares, while A and B were nervously selling.

Yes, dividends were reduced, but a high starting yield provided a strong margin of safety, and even in the darkest of the GFC years, income stayed at levels that were good enough to support the investor’s lifestyle without having to destroy the portfolio’s recovery potential.

You can't predict the next market crash. The sequence of return risk is a problem when there are withdrawals, and it's a killer when the withdrawals are frequent and recurring. Being in a position to buy is always better than being in a position to sell. Buyers have the luxury to be patient and be picky. Sellers tend to get desperate. With a high-yielding portfolio like the one we maintain at High Dividend Opportunities, you can keep collecting income while letting the market do its thing, significantly mitigating the sequence of return risk.

2. Relying on Past Performance in Your Future Plans

Retirees regularly make the mistake of assuming that the future will look like the past and build their plans while looking at the rearview mirror. The problem is that Mr. Market doesn’t care about your plans, charts, or what worked a decade ago. During your working years, you can recover from the negative impact of bad decisions or assumptions. However, in retirement, you don’t get the luxury to start from scratch.

In 1995, the Fed Funds Rate was over 6%. This means someone retiring at this time could easily generate $60,000 annually from a $1 million investment without taking any risk. You may have watched your parents or grandparents do this and enjoy a steady income with relatively low inflation pressures. However, someone retiring in 2003 did not enjoy the same privilege and was stuck with the Fed Funds Rate at 1%, and the situation was worse for someone retiring in 2008. Assuming the same methods from the past will work for you in the future is a poor strategy for a healthy retirement.

Another comparable mistake is to chase what has recently done well. Investors who crowded into the tech sector in the 90s learned this the hard way, with several companies disappearing and notable names taking over 20 years to regain their pre-bust highs. I will note that Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) is still trading below its top tech bubble price levels.

Similarly, investors piled into COVID-19 darlings because remote work enablers were the future, right?

Oh, wasn’t the future going to be all about electric vehicles?

Before you accuse me of taking cheap shots at a few weak themes, let me get into more familiar territory.

Not too long ago, fiber optics was deemed the future of telecommunications, and companies spent billions to lay the infrastructure for it. But demand never caught up, and the cables mostly remained unused for years (decades). Many of those companies that aggressively built them went bankrupt.

Actually, this sentiment goes back centuries. After the Civil War ended, railroad construction became a booming industry, with more than 35,000 miles of track laid between 1866 and 1873, financed heavily by investments from banks and other industries. Railroads were supposed to be the “nation-building” projects, essential for faster and cost-effective transportation of goods across the country. The house of cards began to collapse when Jay Cooke and Company, a U.S. bank, filed for bankruptcy after being overextended, causing the Panic of 1873, termed as the first Great Depression (later changed to Long Depression, after the events in the 1930s eclipsed it).

Today, companies, both large and small, are spending billions to build something they consider the future.

Artificial intelligence…. hmmmm.

But this time it is different?

Past performance provides zero indication of future direction. Just because someone has a flawless driving record for a decade doesn't mean they can't get into an accident tomorrow. Drive responsibly, wear a seat belt, and don't drive under the influence, regardless of how experienced you are. These recommendations are valid in the financial markets, too.

Everything has a price, and if you overpay, it will be at the cost of your financial future.

Conclusion

While you are still in the workforce, you can easily recover from setbacks, such as mistakes or poor planning. As you get closer to retirement, setbacks tend to cause significant derailment, and in retirement, mistakes often send retirees back into the workforce.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we focus on setting up a retirement where income will remain abundant and predictable and capable of tackling the surprises of life. We don’t attempt to invest in the hottest sectors; in fact, we invest in the most uninspiring and boring names you could imagine. Whether it is energy royalties, fresh-shell eggs, utilities, or fixed-income securities, we're collecting massive income from profitable segments of the market. This enables us to be patient buyers during bull, bear, and flat market conditions. This is the power of our Income Method and the beauty of Income Investing.

data.title: Newmont: Upgrading To A Strong Buy As Turnaround Progresses And Gold Surges
data.description:
Introduction

Since I first covered Newmont (NYSE:NEM) back in August and pointed out how their “Turnaround Potential That Still Has Room To Shine,” the stock is up only ~20% now.

This comes despite several massive changes both internally and externally, and I believe they are important enough to warrant an updated valuation, so let’s refresh the thesis with the latest info.

Recent Quarter

Their Q2 was definitely solid, on the back of their execution and especially higher prices of gold, remaining on track to achieve their full year guidance from February 2025. Based on this, their core portfolio is seen producing about 5.6 million ounces at an AISC of about $1,620 and about $3.3 billion in CAPEX (including ramp-ups in H2, as can be seen in the slide above).

This performance resulted in $2.4 billion in cash from operations and $1.7 billion in free cash flow - and that’s with average realized gold prices of $3,320. Simply assuming a 1-for-1 effect to their cash from operations we see in the slide above based on their Q2 report, that’s about $2.9 billion per quarter given the current price of gold. For that FCF, we’re talking about insanely high cash flows in this environment - about $8.3 billion if we annualize the gold-adjusted Q2 results.

To get an idea, in theory, that’s more than the company’s entire debt and would allow them to pay ~8.8% dividend yields (assuming a 100% payout) or, more importantly, expand significantly into whatever they wish. In this environment, gold is… well, gold. The margins on it combined with their streamlined production and potential to expand can shape Newmont’s future.

As I said in the initial analysis, Newmont is undergoing a significant portfolio adjustment plan, selling non-core operations and focusing on developing their Tier One mines. So far, the company is on track to achieve their 5.6 million ounces of gold in 2025 at an AISC of ~1,620/oz while using $1.3 billion in development capital out of $3.2 billion in CAPEX expected this year (see the guidance here).

Back in late September, Newmont also announced that their current President/COO, Natascha Viljoen, will take over as CEO, effective January 1. I guess we could call this the final step in their portfolio’s adjustment, with a refreshed view potentially reigniting Newmont’s acquisition spree or even leading to them developing into more than just gold again, so this is definitely something to keep a close eye on, as it has potential to lead to significant reactions from the market. This could be seen as a positive by the market given NEM’s performance during the (still) current CEO’s tenure, so a turnaround seems more and more likely, especially in this environment.

Macro Developments

Of course, the massive development that everyone is talking about are rate cuts. With two more rate cuts expected in 2025 following the one in September, the market (finally) sees the importance of gold in such an environment. This can also help Newmont with their interest expenses and potential new debt in case they go for that instead of issuing stock, so it’s even better.

As a result of rate cuts and combined with (more) rising uncertainty, gold has now reached over $4,000 per ounce, which is a monumental increase for such a “slow and boring” commodity. However, given the fear around the economy, political uncertainty, rate cuts, etc. potentially weakening the dollar and even risking putting the US in a bad environment, gold seems like an obvious choice.

What I believe happened was the market has been distracted by AI and all kinds of noise, while gold (as well as platinum and silver, which are also up significantly) has been kind of ignored for about half a decade. However, while the market focused on whatever was hot at the time, gold’s fundamentals/role as a precious metal kept brewing, leading to the pent-up demand finally bursting and resulting in what we’re witnessing now, which is literally something we’ll probably talk about for decades.

So, with developments like OPEC+ raising production again for November, oil prices falling to points where US producers have to cut their CAPEX, tariffs leading to significant uncertainty, global political pressure leading to things like China completely stopping soybean orders from the US, which can cause significant disruptions, and more, gold does what it’s supposed to do - hedge us against uncertainty.

Although I prefer gold miners thanks to the leverage they come with, it’s also true that not all of them reacted the same to the metal’s increase, with Newmont being an example. The stock is up about 38% over the past half a decade while the metal itself is up ~110% - nearly 3 times as good. This came despite the company’s acquisitions and developments as a result of their own internal struggles, but with their turnaround in full effect now and very significant portfolio adjustments complete, I believe Newmont still has very significant room to grow - even if gold prices were to fall.

This will ultimately depend on their announcements and earnings, with the Q3 report being scheduled for October 23, with the EPS estimations likely to be crushed thanks to the much better gold prices. The main things to look for in my opinion will be how their portfolio adjustments are going for and what plans will the new CEO have - even better if they hint at a 2026 (or longer) guidance. I believe Newmont has potential to ramp up their acquisitions especially now that they “cleaned” the portfolio, so that is yet another potential catalyst that can help them finally perform as they should, especially since there are so many good peers and projects they can buy, some of which I even rated Strong Buys recently.

They also briefly talked about copper and future projects at the Mining Forum Americas 2025 Conference back in September:

The copper production is organic by its nature and gives us a good organic diversification in the business. It is not something that we will select projects on. Our focus is on projects that is in the right jurisdiction, fit our risk profile and making sure that we deliver the right level of returns.

Updated Valuation

To update the valuation to higher gold prices, we could probably see Newmont making over $6.4 billion in free cash flow in 2025 (this is based on average realized prices of ~$3,650/oz for two more quarters, based on their ramped-up CAPEX of $1.8 billion in H2 and combined with the ~$2.9 billion in FCF recorded in H1). This would then become ~$7.25 billion in 2026 assuming the same gold prices, production and CAPEX, and from that point on I’ll go with a 4% CAGR for the remaining 4 years, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 7.5%, above my estimated 6.9% (assumptions: ~7.2% cost of equity, ~3.8% cost of debt, ~31% corporate tax rate) for a better margin of safety to account for some execution risks, as well as their $1.42 billion in net debt, and 1.098 billion shares outstanding as of Q2. This leads to an Enterprise Value of $144.69 billion, and after deducting net debt, the equity value is $143.27 billion, leading to an intrinsic value per share of $130.48.

Conclusions

Overall, given their strong and improving fundamentals, portfolio rebalancing, macro tailwinds and potential opportunity coming from the CEO change, I will upgrade Newmont to a Strong Buy. Execution risk remains, but Newmont still seems undervalued even if gold prices fall, while higher prices combined with their very solid production as the leader of the industry significantly offset the risks, in my opinion.

data.title: The REIT Comeback Is Taking Shape
data.description:
The big event of the past month was the Federal Reserve’s decision to cut interest rates, marking the first cut of this year.

In their commentary, they noted that the job market was weakening and implied that further rate cuts may be needed in the near term. The Fed Chair, Jerome Powell, was very measured as usual in his remarks, but the Fed Governor, Michelle Bowman, was a lot clearer. Here is what she said:

"It's a lot easier to support the labor market by lowering the federal funds rate than it is to fix it after it's broken. It is time for the Committee to act decisively and proactively to address decreasing labor market dynamism and emerging signs of fragility." (emphasis added)

As a result, the debt market is now expecting two additional rate cuts by the end of this year:

And another three cuts in 2026, potentially bringing interest rates all the way down to 3% by the end of next year:

This might be concerning for some sectors of the market, as a weakening economy could lead to lower profits.

However, in the case of REITs, this is very good news because the positive impact of rate cuts is expected to be much greater than the negative impact of a weaker economy.

Most REITs (VNQ) are just not very cyclical. They generally own recession-resistant properties such as apartment communities, cell towers, net lease properties, data centers, e-commerce warehouses, healthcare properties, or even grocery stores. These properties are typically leased on a long-term basis with contractually agreed rent hikes for many years to come. For this reason, rents tend to rise even during recessions. It is only a minority of REITs that own offices, hotels, and other cyclical assets. Therefore, the impact of a weakening economy is not significant on most REITs.

However, the positive impact of rate cuts could be very significant because REITs are today still priced at near-decade low valuations after suffering a 3-year-long bear market that was almost entirely driven by the Fed’s rate hikes of 2022 and 2023:

Fundamentally, the rate hikes did not have much of an impact on most REITs as their leverage is at an all-time low of just about 35%. However, REITs crashed anyway because the rate hikes pulled a lot of capital out of the REIT sector into fixed-income investments.

Just look at how much money poured into money-market funds alone. Some of it came from REITs:

But as interest rates are now cut, we expect the opposite to happen, with some of that capital making its way back into REITs.

And you don’t have to take it from me.

The Fed began cutting interest rates in late 2023, and this has already pushed REITs about 40% higher as investors reallocated more heavily to them:

We are simply predicting that this recovery will continue as interest rates are cut down another 5 times over the next year, as even more investors will reallocate larger portions of their capital into REITs.

Money market funds and short-term treasuries were attractive to investors when they were offering 5% yields, but as interest rates are cut down to just 3% or less, even as inflation remains elevated and uncertain, I expect REITs to become a far more attractive option in the eyes of most investors, relatively speaking.

Ask yourself this question: Would you rather earn a 3% yield from a money market fund, which, after inflation and taxes, leaves you with a negative real return, or would you prefer to buy a 6% yielding REIT that enjoys steady growth, strong inflation protection, and could enjoy additional upside as more investors reallocate into REITs?

If you agree that REITs are becoming increasingly attractive, relatively speaking, then now is the time to allocate to them ahead of the crowd while valuations still remain low, yields are high, and many present significant upside potential.

The opportunity to buy blue-chip REITs like NNN REIT (NNN) at a near 6% dividend yield could be gone by this time next year. It has a BBB+ rated balance sheet, a 36-year track record of steady dividend growth, a clear path for steady growth for years to come, and yet, it still trades at a historically high yield.

But as yields on money-market funds and short-term treasuries land in the high 2s or low 3s, NNN REIT's near 6% dividend yield will stand out a lot more, attracting a lot more capital, likely pushing it a lot higher. Repricing at a 4.5% dividend yield, which is more in line with its historical average, would result in about 20% upside from here, and while you wait, you earn the high yield, and the REIT keeps growing its cash flow and value. All of it combined has the potential to earn you 15%+ average annual total returns for the next 3-5 years. An amazing risk-to-reward in my opinion, coming from such a defensive business.

Best of all, NNN is far from being an exception, so you can build a diversified portfolio of similar REITs trading at low valuations and offering high dividend yields, preparing for this catalyst to push them a lot higher.

At High Yield Landlord, our plan for the last quarter of this year is to further consolidate our capital towards our best ideas to maximize gains in this anticipated recovery.

So far this year, we have sold 8 of our holdings to reinvest in others, and we expect to do more of the same over the coming months.

REITs are finally winning again, up 40% over the past 2 years alone, and now is not the time to slow down, right as the catalyst we have long been waiting for is about to unfold.

data.title: FBTC: Powerful Drivers Align
data.description:
Precious metals have been grabbing most of the headlines in recent months as they surge higher, but it's also been a solid year for Bitcoin and the Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund ETF (BATS:FBTC), which made a new all-time high in October. This article looks at why Bitcoin and FBTC should continue higher over the medium and long term as several powerful drivers align.

1. Fiat Concerns, Dovish Banks and Lower Rates

My last article on FBTC was published back in May and titled "Breaking Out On Fiat Fears." To summarize, Moody's had just downgraded the US credit rating, further eroding trust in the US dollar. This ignited a strong move in FBTC to all-time highs, as Bitcoin is an alternative to fiat currency.

Since then, the situation for the US dollar, and for other global currencies, has worsened. President Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill" passed and is set to deepen fiscal deficits, at least over the next few years, until the US government can grow its finances sufficiently (an optimistic view).

More recently, the Fed shifted dovish and resumed its cutting cycle.

A cut in September initially led to a brief drop in Bitcoin, but it has since recovered and made new all-time highs in October. More cuts are on the way, with 25bps in September nearly fully priced, 25bps in December around 80% likely according to the CME FedWatch tool. That may just be the start. We now have Trump pick Steve Miran as a new governor, and he is advocating for aggressive cuts towards the low 2% area. That is around 2% lower. And when current Chair Powell is replaced in May, he will almost certainly be replaced by a dove to guide rates towards this goal.

The dovish shift in the Fed highlights that the Fed will ease at the slightest hint of economic weakness. They have framed the shift as "risk management," but unemployment of 4.3% is not necessarily a level to make a series of cuts, especially when inflation is on the rise.

The apparent abandonment of the inflation side of the Fed's mandate is particularly positive for FBTC. Bitcoin is seen as an inflation hedge. Furthermore, the divisions in the Fed (the recent meeting had very divided opinions on where rates should be in December) and upcoming Chair change could lead to a muddled policy. I already think the Fed may be making a policy mistake by cutting aggressively just when inflation is a risk again.

Taken together, the rate cuts, the rise in inflation, and the lack of cohesion at the Fed all spell trouble for the US dollar and make alternative currencies more attractive. And, of course, Bitcoin is a global asset, and global currencies have their own domestic negatives. The ECB has cut 8 times, and just look at the Swiss National Bank, which has cut back to 0% and has nowhere to go but negative rates again. If the US economy should finally fall back in a recession, the Fed could do the same.

In any case, the trend is clear: rates are heading lower.

Lower rates make alternative assets like Bitcoin and gold more attractive. If yields are at 5%, bonds may be seen as a safe place to park some cash and earn modest interest. If yields are 2%, bonds are no longer keeping up with inflation, and the investment no longer makes sense. The money is forced to look elsewhere.

2. Powerful Support

It's becoming easier to buy and own crypto, thanks to the launch of ETFs like FBTC and deregulation. The Trump administration is crypto-friendly and swiftly pushed through the CLARITY Act, the GENIUS Act, and established a federal "Strategic Bitcoin Reserve." The latter stopped short of actually buying crypto, but it may be a matter of time. Earlier this week, Deutsche Bank suggested central banks will end up holding Bitcoin:

A strategic Bitcoin allocation could emerge as a modern cornerstone of financial security, echoing gold’s role in the 20th century. Assessing volatility, liquidity, strategic value and trust, we find that both assets will likely feature on central bank balance sheets by 2030.

Central banks are not buying right now, but the prospect of this in the future is a reason to be optimistic. We only have to look at gold's move in 2024 and 2025 to see what central bank buying can do.

In the meantime, FBTC can benefit from the institutional rush to buy.

With a friendlier regulatory environment under the Trump administration, nearly 60% of institutional investors plan to boost their crypto allocation in the coming year, and average exposure is expected to more than double within three years, according to State Street's annual digital assets and emerging technology survey released on Thursday.

Institutions are late to the crypto party and are underinvested. State Street continued,

The average portfolio allocation across a range of digital assets stood at 7%, the survey found, and that's expected to jump to 16% by 2028.

This will be powerful support.

Indeed, Bitcoin has a lot of powerful supporters.

With strong institutional buying, the potential for central bank buying, and the President of the US on your side, the long-term outlook looks positive.

FBTC Basics

FBTC has been somewhat eclipsed by the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), which has grown its AUM to $97B.

This isn't necessarily a reflection of better quality. There is almost no difference between the two funds. They both have an expense ratio of 0.25% and the returns are almost identical.

I think it may have something to do with BlackRock's iShares series being more visible and well-known. When given the choice between two brands, most people choose the one they know best.

One consideration is that FBTC and IBIT have different custodians, and Fidelity is a self-custodian. If you are worried about safety, it may be wise to spread assets between both funds.

Risks

Bitcoin is a volatile asset and has experienced several major bear markets in the last decade. The last one was in 2021-2022 when Bitcoin dropped 77%.

While the outlook in 2025 looks better than it was in 2021, another large drop can't be ruled out. I would be particularly careful if the trend from the 2022 low breaks.

The 2021 highs around $65,000 might be tested in a major bear market, but I am doubtful any drop would get this far, and I would view it as a great buying opportunity.

Conclusions

2025 has been a great year for crypto, Bitcoin and for FBTC. The downgrade to US government debt and the dovish shift by the Fed, despite the backdrop of rising inflation, make Bitcoin attractive compared to fiat-backed assets. Furthermore, deregulation and a supportive President have led to an institutional rush to buy. At some point, central banks may join the buying frenzy.

The FBTC outlook for the long-term, therefore, looks better than ever as powerful drivers align. That doesn't mean the path higher will be smooth, but it does mean that dips should be seen as buying opportunities.

data.title: Alliance Laundry: Do Not Get Washed
data.description:
Shares of Alliance Laundry Holdings (NYSE:ALH) have been shooting higher after its IPO as investors like the prospects for the commercial laundry producer here.

I like the revenue and margin track record, yet expectations and leverage are a bit high here, too high for me to see appeal from current levels going forwards.

Every Day is Laundry Day

Alliance Laundry Holdings is a system manufacturer that provides best-in-class reliability quality and delivers on the best total cost of ownership. The company designs, produces, and sells commercial laundry systems with a complete focus on such solutions.

The global market for commercial, residential, and industrial laundry systems is pegged in excess of $80 billion per annum, with the commercial market size seen in excess of $7 billion. It is this segment that the company is focusing on. This +$7 billion segment in itself is comprised of sub-segments, which include vended (laundromats), OPL (on-premise laundry), aftermarket, and commercial-in-home segments.

The company focuses heavily on quality and claims that the capital investments made by the business are just a fraction of the total cost of ownership, which is determined by utility consumption, service & repair costs, maintenance, cleaning, loss of productivity, and use of detergents.

With quality coming at a price, the company claims that its quality offerings are worth it, as they reduce operating expenses down the road, supporting superior economics. This is key for clientele seen in hotels, healthcare, laundromats, and others.

The company has a huge network of some 600 distributors, which allows for the sale and distribution of such products, with the business generating about three-quarters of sales in the US home markets, complemented by international markets, which are still relatively smaller.

The company was acquired by its principal shareholder back in 2015, who is now looking to partially cash in a decade later.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Alliance Laundry Holdings aimed to sell 34.1 million shares in a preliminary price range between $19 and $22 per share, with final pricing set at the high end of the range, following solid demand for its offering. Note that some 24.4 million shares were sold by the company, with the remainder of the shares sold by selling shareholders.

As a result, Alliance raised gross proceeds of around $537 million with the offering, excluding the money raised by selling shareholders, and of course all ahead of exercising the over-allotment option.

With over 197 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $4.3 billion equity valuation at the offer price. This excludes a net debt position of around $2.4 billion ahead of the offering, with the proceeds from the offering pushing down net debt to around $1.9 billion. This would give the company a $4.3 billion enterprise valuation.

After posting flattish sales in 2023, the company grew 2024 sales by more than 10% to $1.51 billion. Operating margins were very healthy, with operating profits of $284 million translating into margins at around 19% of sales. The company incurred a big $132 million interest expense bill in 2024, yet proceeds from the offering might reduce the interest bill a bit going forward.

Momentum has furthermore been very strong in the first half of 2025, with revenues up nearly 15% to $837 million as operating margins hit 20% of sales. At this rate, the company is generating about $1.7 billion in sales and some $340 million in operating earnings.

With adjusted EBITDA reported at $219 million in the first half of the year, the annualised numbers still work down to a leverage ratio around 4.3 times. Assuming that annual interest expenses might fall to about $100 million going forward, and assuming a 25% tax rate, earnings might come in around $0.90 per share. That implies that at the offer price of $22, the earnings multiples come in at the mid-twenties, with leverage ratios exceeding 4 times!

All this has risen a bit further with shares trading at $24 and change, as that move added another half a billion to the valuation and thus expectations, pushing up multiples and expectations a bit further here.

Concluding Thoughts

The reality is that Alliance Laundry looks like a very decent business, one with growing revenues and fat and stable margins, driven by superior product quality and sound business operations. However, the main risk that I see is that of high valuations in the mid-twenties, also with quite some leverage taken on, as the combination of that is the main risk in my view.

This is also illustrated by a more than 3 times sales multiple applied to the business, while a peer like Whirlpool (WHR) trades far below 1 times sales, although for good reasons, being hurt by debt, a poor margin performance, and poor execution at large.

Other risks include the normal business risks related to tariffs, trading policies, competition, suppliers, etc., as the main risk is the one described above. That is the biggest risk that I fear, that is, investors being washed here.

While I believe that quality and reputation are key, certainly in markets hurt by labor shortages and expensive labor rates, I feel no need to chase the shares here, certainly not given the momentum being displayed around the public offering.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: A Safe Haven That's Quietly Becoming A Bit Overpriced
data.description:
In April 2025, I published my last article about Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ), and I rated the stock as a 'Buy.' In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

I would argue that Johnson & Johnson can be seen as fairly valued, and it is certainly not the perfect investment. But in a time of very high levels of uncertainty, limited stability and high volatility on the stock market, Johnson & Johnson might be a business worth considering.

I still am not expecting high-growth rates from Johnson & Johnson in the years to come, and the stock is certainly not a stock likely to double or triple in a few years. But I consider the stock as an investment that will provide some level of stability in volatile times and an investment with limited downside risk. Johnson & Johnson, as one of the major healthcare and pharmaceutical businesses might be more recession-resilient than many other businesses and over the next few years, this might actually be one of the most important criteria for any investment.

In my last article, I was cautiously optimistic and called Johnson & Johnson certainly not the best investment. I also pointed out that we should not expect high returns for Johnson & Johnson, but in the meantime the stock performed quite well. It increased 23% since April 2025 (and, when including dividends, the return was almost 25%). However, Johnson & Johnson still underperformed the S&P 500, which increased 25.5%.

Before Johnson & Johnson reports third-quarter results next Tuesday, October 14th, 2025, we look at the stock and company once again and try to determine whether Johnson & Johnson is still a good investment. And we start by looking at the chart, as the stock seems to be at a decisive point right now.

Chart

Johnson & Johnson was in an extended correction since April 2022, when the stock hit its all-time high, which was only succeeded in the last few days. Since April 2022, Johnson & Johnson was in a corrective pattern, but in the last few months the stock moved sharply higher and not only reached its previous all-time high again but was able to move even a bit higher.

The question right now is whether Johnson & Johnson can move higher in the coming weeks or if it remains within its corrective pattern (and below $190). We should point out that Johnson & Johnson moved sharply higher - from almost $140 to $190 in only a few months. While many other stocks make similar moves in much shorter timeframes, it seems like a big move for Johnson & Johnson, and a correction in the coming weeks seems more likely.

In my opinion, the upcoming third-quarter results will have a huge impact on the direction in which the stock will move. With a positive surprise and better-than-expected earnings, the stock might actually continue its rally, but in my opinion a correction seems more likely.

Second Quarter Results

Before we look forward to what to expect for Johnson & Johnson in the coming quarters, we look at the last quarterly results, which were solid. Sales increased from $22,447 million in Q2/24 to $23,743 million in Q2/25, resulting in 5.8% year-over-year growth. Earnings before provision for taxes on income increased 12.9% year-over-year from $5,748 million in the same quarter last year to $6,491 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $1.93 in Q2/24 to $2.29 in Q2/25, resulting in 18.7% year-over-year growth.

Aside from reporting solid second-quarter results, Johnson & Johnson also raised its full-year guidance, and operational sales are now expected to be between $92.7 billion and $93.1 billion, resulting in 4.5% to 5.0% growth compared to last year. Adjusted earnings per share are now expected to be between $10.80 and $10.90 for the full year resulting in 8.2% to 9.2% year-over-year growth.

Growth: Next Quarter and Beyond?

For the third quarter, analysts are expecting revenue to be $23.74 billion (and compared to $22.47 billion in revenue in the same quarter, this is an increase of 5.7% year-over-year). Earnings per share are estimated to be $2.30, and compared to the same quarter last year, this would result in almost doubling earnings per share. However, when looking at earnings per share in the last few years, we see heavy fluctuations, which are not only making year-over-year comparisons rather difficult but are also not a good sign for the business.

In the last 90 days, analysts were getting more optimistic. While only 2 analysts lowered estimates, 15 analysts increased their estimates for earnings per share. And analysts are also optimistic for the long run. During the recent Bernstein 2nd Annual Global Healthcare Conference, the company's Group Chairman for Innovative Medicine in North America stated:

I'll just end and say, at the end of the day, we believe we're in another era of growth where exciting opportunities are ahead of us. We feel more than confident we're going to be able to deliver the 5% to 7% compound annual growth rate, even in the upper limit due to this transformational medical innovation that we're bringing, and we're excited to be there.

And he mentioned 5% to 7% growth annually several times and was especially pointing towards the high-growth potential for the company's oncology business:

We do believe in oncology, by 2030, we're going to see a $50 billion peak year sales really based on the backbone of our multiple myeloma franchise. So I feel more than confident we're going to be able to live into the 5% to 7% and achieve more than what we expect.

Considering that oncology was generating about $20.8 billion in sales in 2024, growing to $50 billion over six years is resulting in an annual growth rate above 15% and would certainly have a huge impact on Johnson & Johnson's overall business and top line.

And this would probably lead to high growth rates for the overall business as well, but analysts don't seem to be as optimistic as management at this point. For the next ten years, earnings per share are estimated to grow with a CAGR of 3.48%.

Between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2030, analysts are more optimistic and expect a CAGR of 7.58% for the bottom line.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

The increasing stock price in the last few months was going hand in hand with expanding valuation multiples, as the stock price increase was not really driven by a fundamental improvement but rather by sentiment becoming more optimistic.

At the time of writing, Johnson & Johnson is trading for 20 times earnings and 25 times free cash flow.

But in both cases, the metrics are below the 5-year average (I excluded previous data as the P/E ratio was fluctuating heavily before). Based on these valuation multiples, it is rather difficult to answer the question of whether Johnson & Johnson is a good investment, but I would rather argue that 25 times free cash flow is too expensive for a business that is rather struggling to grow.

As always, we also use a discount cash flow calculation to calculate an intrinsic value for the stock so we can combine the results from the simple valuation multiples with the results from the DCF calculation.

At first, let's calculate a WACC for Johnson & Johnson by using typical assumptions. We use a market risk premium of 6% and a risk-free rate in line with the 10-year treasury yield (4.129%). All other metrics are taken from the last filings or current market data. Using these numbers, we get a WACC of only 5.09% for Johnson & Johnson.

When calculating with a WACC (or discount rate) of only 5.09% and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (2,419 million), as well as the free cash flow of the last four quarters ($18,180 million), Johnson & Johnson would have to grow only slightly above 1% to be fairly valued right now.

Of course, I see reasons for using such a WACC calculation (not every market environment is different and has an impact on valuation multiples, and not every industry or every business is the same). Nevertheless, I still will calculate with my fixed 10% discount rate (unless the calculated WACC is clearly higher and the risk profile would demand a much higher discount rate) to make different stocks more comparable. In the end, I still can decide whether I want to invest in a business with rather mediocre expected returns or a company with high returns but maybe a riskier profile.

Using a discount rate of 10%, Johnson & Johnson must grow its free cash flow 10% annually for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth until perpetuity. We might be able to argue that Johnson & Johnson is a high-quality company and can continue to grow at a higher pace even ten years from now. But in both scenarios, Johnson & Johnson must grow at a rather high pace, and I honestly don't see Johnson & Johnson growing either in the high single digits or even double digits in the next few years, and I also don't see the company growing 6% in perpetuity. Neither analysts' estimates for the years to come nor past results support such a growth assumption.

Instead, I would see 5% growth annually as a reasonable assumption for Johnson & Johnson, and assuming all else is equal, this leads to an intrinsic value of $150.31, making Johnson & Johnson a bit overvalued again. And it would be rather cautious at this point, as Johnson & Johnson (hand in hand with other pharmaceutical companies) are facing several risks and a high level of uncertainty, including tariffs and the pressure to lower drug prices.

Tariffs and High Drug Prices

Although it was not dominating the news in the last few days, we should not ignore the announcement Donald Trump made about two weeks ago: 100% tariffs on branded and patented drugs starting October 1, 2025. Back then, exemptions for companies actively building new manufacturing facilities in the United States were also announced. On October 1st, it was announced that Trump is holding off on pharma tariffs to enable companies to negotiate deals with the administration. One day earlier, the pharmaceutical giant Pfizer (PFE) announced an agreement to offer all brand-name drugs marketed by the company at lower prices to the U.S. consumers.

The question now is how much Johnson & Johnson might be affected here. For starters, about 36% of the company's revenue is generated from its MedTech business and should therefore not be affected by 100% tariffs on pharmaceuticals. Back in April 2025, executives at Johnson & Johnson stated that tariffs might put a $400 million dent in the company's financial forecast. In the meantime, Johnson & Johnson raised its guidance again, and the situation is very volatile - hence, these predictions might not be accurate anymore.

Additionally, Johnson & Johnson might be one of the companies being exempted, as they have already broken ground in March 2025 on a major biologics facility in Wilson, North Carolina. The $2 billion investment was announced in October 2024 already. So far, I also could not find any information about management negotiating a deal with the administration, as the investments might be enough to be exempt already.

On the other hand, we should not ignore that lowering drug prices is one of the major goals for the Trump Administration. In May 2025, the White House released an executive order, the Most Favored Nation Policy, in which it declared a war on high drug prices, and although one could and should look a little deeper at the reasons, it is true that the healthcare system in the United States is expensive (and compared to other countries, not as effective as it could be, measured by the lower life expectancy).

It is also true that drug prices in the United States are extremely high, and it is very difficult to justify that premium. In 2022, drug prices for prescription drugs in the United States were 2.78 times the price of all other countries. This is including brands and generics; when only looking at brand drugs, prices were at least 3.22 times as high as in comparison countries. Drug prices were taken from 2022, and the study was released in January 2024 (under the Biden administration, as previous presidents have also criticized high drug prices).

While Johnson & Johnson might be exempted from potential tariffs due to the construction of a new manufacturing site in the United States, we don't know if pharmaceutical companies won't be put under additional pressure to lower drug prices.

Additional Risks

Stelara sales already declined steeply in the second quarter (almost 43%), and in the first half of 2025, sales declined about 39% compared to the first half of 2024. During the Bernstein 2nd Annual Global Healthcare Conference, Tom Cavanaugh was quite optimistic that losses from Stelara can be offset by the company. He stated:

I think as many of people had realized, we were facing the loss of exclusivity of STELARA, the biggest asset that we had in Johnson & Johnson. And I would tell you, that's behind us. I think we've proven ourselves to be able to what we set out to do is grow through loss of exclusivity of STELARA in the coming years. In the first 2 quarters, we've proven just that.

Despite this optimism, there is still the risk of Stelara sales declining more steeply than expected, and, of course, the risk of a recession in the United States is still lingering. But I already talked about this in my last article and don't want to repeat the arguments made.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Johnson & Johnson is not the best investment right now - neither for the short-term nor for the long-term. Over the long run, I see Johnson & Johnson generating "only" returns in the mid-to-high single digits, and we have to take into account the risks mentioned above, which could lower margins and profits.

In the short term, I see a correction being likely, and I don't think Johnson & Johnson will be able to really break above the previous all-time high. But a lot will depend on the third-quarter results the company is reporting next week. Especially in the short term, sentiment is driving the stock market, and surprising earnings (either better or worse than expected) might drive the stock price in one way or the other. I will remain on the sidelines as I still don't see Johnson & Johnson being a good long-term investment, and other names from the pharmaceutical sector are probably better plays.
